claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 19 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 19 and in approved education
  - Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
circumstances: Child is 19 and in approved education
Another claimant who lives with the child has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 11 weeks (within 12-week limit)
Child works 24+ hours/week but is in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for:**

How old is the child (or children) you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know their age, and if you have multiple children, please tell me the ages of each child you'd like to claim for.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my son Alex - he's 19 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Since Alex is 19 years old, he falls into the **16 to 19 age category**. For children in this age range, eligibility depends on whether they're in approved education or training.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is Alex currently in approved education or training?**

This could include:
- **In education or training** – such as A-levels, T-levels, Scottish Highers, NVQs (up to level 3), or similar qualifications
- **Recently left education** – if Alex has left education or training within the last few months, there may be an extension period

Which best describes Alex's situation?

1. **Currently in education or training**
2. **Recently left education or training** (within the last few months)
3. **Neither** – not in education/training and hasn't been recently

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's currently in education - he's studying for his A-levels.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! Alex is currently in education studying for his A-levels. Let me check the education level requirements.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! A-levels are on the list of **approved qualifications** for Child Benefit purposes. I just need to confirm a few more details about Alex's studies:

1. **Is Alex studying full-time?** (This means at least 12 hours per week of supervised study/course time)

2. **Is his education funded by an employer**, or does he have an agreement with an employer to work for them after finishing?

Please let me know these details so we can continue the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he's studying full-time. And no, it's not funded by an employer - he's just doing his A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is studying A-levels full-time at college with no employer funding. That meets the approved education criteria.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Now I need to check Alex's work situation:

**Does Alex work less than 24 hours per week?** (Or is he not in paid work at all?)

If he has a part-time job alongside his studies, that's fine as long as it's under 24 hours per week. Working 24 hours or more per week would affect eligibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He does work - he's got a full-time job at the supermarket alongside his studies.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thanks for letting me know. I need to clarify what "full-time" means in this context, as it's important for eligibility.

**How many hours per week does Alex work at the supermarket?**

The key threshold is **24 hours per week**:
- **Less than 24 hours per week** – this is fine, he can still qualify for Child Benefit
- **24 hours or more per week** – this would affect his eligibility

Do you know roughly how many hours he works each week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, he works over 24 hours a week - it's a proper full-time job.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming that. Unfortunately, this does affect Alex's eligibility.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry, but based on the information you've provided, **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex** at this time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## Summary: Not Eligible for Child Benefit

**Child:** Alex (age 19)

**Result:** ❌ **Not Eligible**

**Reason:** Although Alex is in approved education (studying A-levels full-time at college), he is working **24 or more hours per week**. Under Child Benefit rules, young people aged 16 or over who work full-time (24+ hours per week) are not eligible for Child Benefit.

---

### What you can do:

- **If Alex's work hours reduce** to less than 24 hours per week in the future while he continues his studies, you may be able to claim Child Benefit again at that point.

- **If Alex finishes his A-levels** and continues into further approved education or training (that isn't advanced education like a university degree), and works fewer hours, you could potentially claim again.

---

I understand this may not be the news you were hoping for. Is there anything else I can help you with, or do you have any questions about this decision?

reasonings: Alex is 19 years old and is studying A-levels full-time, which would normally be approved education. However, he is also working 24 or more hours per week at a supermarket. Under Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24+ hours per week are not eligible, regardless of whether they are also in education.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex because, while Alex is in approved education (A-levels), he is working 24 or more hours per week. The rules state that young people aged 16-19 working full-time (24+ hours) are not eligible for Child Benefit.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.reason: Child aged 16+ is working 24 or more hours per week and not in approved education or training
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops if child aged 16 or over starts paid work for 24+ hours per week and is no longer in approved education or training. If they return to education/training, you may be able to claim again.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 19 years old and is studying A-levels full-time, which would normally be approved education. However, he is also working 24 or more hours per week at a supermarket. Under Child Benefit rules, children aged 16 or over who work 24+ hours per week are not eligible, regardless of whether they are also in education.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for their son Alex because, while Alex is in approved education (A-levels), he is working 24 or more hours per week. The rules state that young people aged 16-19 working full-time (24+ hours) are not eligible for Child Benefit.
